items.q: Does light always travel at the same speed?
items.correct: Only in a vacuum; in any material medium (water, glass) light travels more slowly, which is what causes refraction.
items.misconception: Yes — c is a universal constant in every situation.
items.random_wrong: No — light always slows with distance from its source.
items.why: Students take 'c is constant' as 'c everywhere' and miss the qualifier 'in a vacuum'.

items.q: What determines the energy of a single photon?
items.correct: Its frequency (E = hf) — only frequency, not intensity.
items.misconception: A brighter light has higher-energy photons because intensity makes each photon more energetic.
items.random_wrong: Photon energy depends on how fast the photon is moving relative to the source.
items.why: Students conflate intensity (number of photons) with photon energy (frequency-dependent); this is why dim UV is more dangerous than bright red.

items.q: In which direction does an electric field point near a positive charge?
items.correct: Radially outwards — the field is defined by the force on a positive test charge, which is repelled.
items.misconception: Radially inwards because positive attracts negative.
items.random_wrong: There is no field around a positive charge unless another charge is present.
items.why: Students confuse the convention (positive test charge) and forget that an isolated positive source still has a field even without another charge.

items.q: A car travels 4 km north then 3 km east. What is its displacement magnitude?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 5 km. “7 km” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Kinetic energy. “Acceleration” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
